summary: Spolsky presents a comprehensive guide to user interface design aimed at programmers, built around one central axiom: a UI is well-designed when the program behaves exactly how the user expected it to. Drawing from psychology research on learned helplessness, he argues that users' happiness depends on feeling in control of their environment, and that tiny frustrations from unexpected behavior accumulate into genuine unhappiness. He covers practical principles including matching the program model to the user model, minimizing unnecessary choices, using affordances and metaphors, maintaining consistency with platform conventions, designing for users who don't read and can't precisely control the mouse, and using activity-based planning. The book concludes with a six-step design process: invent imaginary users, identify activities, understand user models, sketch designs, iterate, and test with real humans.
key_points: The cardinal axiom of UI design is that a program should behave exactly how the user expected it to, based on their mental model formed from prior experience with other software and real-world analogies.

Every option you provide forces the user to make a decision — designers should make hard choices themselves rather than abdicating responsibility by exposing configuration that users don't care about.

Consistency with platform conventions matters more than creativity or correctness — millions of users trained on existing software will expect your program to work the same way, and deviating from that causes real frustration.
Users don't read instructions, can't precisely control the mouse, and can't remember things — design must accommodate these constraints rather than assuming attentive, skilled, motivated users.

Activity-based planning (designing around what users are trying to accomplish rather than around feature lists) produces dramatically better software, as demonstrated by Excel 5.0's discovery that most users just kept lists.
money_quotes: A user interface is well-designed when the program behaves exactly how the user thought it would.
Every time you provide an option, you're asking the user to make a decision.
It is the height of arrogance for a software designer to inflict a choice like this on the user simply because the designer couldn't think hard enough to decide which option is really better.
Your emotions don't seem to care about the magnitude of the event, only the quality.
The more you feel that you can control your environment, and that the things you do are actually working, the happier you are.
Being inconsistent is almost always more work than just acting like your platform expects you to act.

Using a command line interface is like having to learn Korean to order food in a Seoul branch of McDonalds. Using a menu based interface is like being able to point to the food you want and nod your head vigorously.
There is a much worse kind of arrogance in software design: the arrogant assumption that 'my software is so damn cool, people are just going to have to warp their brains around it.'
key_insight: User happiness stems from feeling in control of their environment, so UI design must prioritize matching the user's mental model over technical elegance, creative expression, or feature richness.
